title: Electromotive Force and Potential Difference — Medium

questions.question: A charge of $4.0 \times 10^{4}\text{ C}$ flows through an electric heater. If $8.0\text{ MJ}$ of electrical energy is converted into thermal energy, what is the potential difference across the heater?
questions.explanation:
Potential difference $V = W / Q$. $8.0\text{ MJ}$ is $8.0 \times 10^{6}\text{ J}$. Thus, $V = (8.0 \times 10^{6}) / (4.0 \times 10^{4}) = 200\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A current of $0.50\text{ A}$ flows through a bulb for $2.0\text{ minutes}$. If the potential difference across the bulb is $12\text{ V}$, how much energy is dissipated?
questions.explanation:
First, find charge $Q = I \times t = 0.50\text{ A} \times 120\text{ s} = 60\text{ C}$. Then, $W = V \times Q = 12\text{ V} \times 60\text{ C} = 720\text{ J}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between electromotive force (e.m.f.) and potential difference (p.d.)?
options: e.m.f. relates to energy supplied by the source; p.d. relates to energy used by a component
e.m.f. is measured in Newtons; p.d. is measured in Volts
e.m.f. is used for parallel circuits; p.d. is used for series circuits
e.m.f. is the total current; p.d. is the current through one branch
questions.explanation:
According to the sources, e.m.f. is the work done by a source to move a unit charge around a circuit, while p.d. is the work done by a unit charge passing through a specific component.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student measures the potential difference across a resistor using an analogue voltmeter. If the work done to move $2.0\text{ C}$ of charge through the resistor is $10\text{ J}$, what should the voltmeter read?
questions.explanation:
The reading on the voltmeter is the potential difference $V = W / Q = 10\text{ J} / 2.0\text{ C} = 5.0\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: One volt is equivalent to which of the following combinations of units?
questions.explanation:
Since $V = W / Q$ (energy per unit charge), the volt is defined as one joule per coulomb.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
